title_candidates.text: He Won at the Supreme Court — and Still Went Back to Prison | Miranda Rights
How 4 Sentences Rewrote Every Arrest in America | Miranda v. Arizona

description:
"You have the right to remain silent." You've heard it in a thousand movies. But it didn't come from a screenwriter — it came from one Supreme Court ruling in 1966, and from a man who won his case at the highest court in the country and still ended up back in prison.

In this episode, we trace exactly how that happened: from a 1963 arrest in Phoenix, to a contested confession, to a 5–4 Supreme Court decision that built a small but permanent fix into every interrogation in America.

────────────────────────────────
📌 CHAPTERS
────────────────────────────────
0:00 Hook — The Sentence You Know
0:23 What the Warning Really Is
1:04 Phoenix, Arizona, 1963
1:30 Inside the Interrogation Room
2:10 The Legal Question the Law Started Asking
2:44 The Case Climbs to the Supreme Court
3:25 Four Cases, One Problem
4:35 June 13, 1966 — The Decision
4:55 The Four Rights, Spelled Out
5:50 The Dissent
6:09 How the Warning Became Routine
7:12 The Twist — What Happened to Ernesto Miranda
8:22 The Shape of the Whole Thing
9:10 Why They Still Have to Read You Your Rights

────────────────────────────────
Prime Documentary covers landmark legal cases, constitutional history, and the hidden systems that shape everyday American life — one deep-dive at a time.

Next episode: How a single case changed who gets a lawyer in the first place.
